sentences.text: Closer to-- to the Second World War.
My brother was at that time in Israel.
And in the-- in the years between 1935 and '36, he wrote to my mother that the times-- it was already after my father passed away, and we notified him.
My father passed away in 1935.
He had colon cancer and it metastasized to the bones.
And my sister left Vienna, where she was studying languages, and she came home to help my mother.
She had-- she was in the store with her, but mainly, she took my father to-- to visit doctors.
And, of course, nothing.
They weren't able to do anything.
And he passed away.
Apparently it was a heart attack.
In 1935 he passed away.
Far away-- far away.
After Rowne it was Warsaw, and after Warsaw were-- were even around Krakow.
Yes.
She traveled with him and visited the doctors.
Possible that the diagnosis was made right away, and possibly not because he was-- he was treated as a sciatic problem, having a sciatic problem.
And that was when the bones were-- when it metastasized to the bones.
So he passed away.
My brother was at that time in Israel.
He was not notified by telegram, he was notified by a letter that he has to say kaddish.
In the meantime, I went to schule.
I was 10 at that time.
And I said kaddish after my father.
[SOBBING] My husband's father was always nearby, and he would, you know, tell me when-- when is my turn to say.
Of course, Hebrew I knew.

And in those years, my brother already encouraged my mother to sell the business and the house and come to Israel because he had refugees from Germany, and they were scared and worried, and he related to her that the times are coming.
That-- he was always a pessimist, but he was right this time.
And he said, come, take Paula and take Leah and Sonia and come to-- to Israel.
But my mother was a little bit on the stubborn side and she said, am I going to go and-- and-- and be dependent on the children?
You know, make a living?
I can't make a living there.
And they remained in the town of Hoszcza.
And the war between-- between '36 and '39, there was an exceptional influx of anti-Semitism.
German people that lived in our areas were taught that they were the ones that showed the Germans where to bomb.
Not the villages, but the middle of the town of Hoszcza.
In 1939, you know, with the blitzkrieg, in no time, Poland lost the war.
And the Russian occupied in 1941, made a pact with Hitler, and occupied our town of Hoszcza.
And the eastern part up to the Hoszcza, up to Warsaw was, belonged to-- to the-- to the Soviet Union, and the German had the other western part of Poland-- south, west, and north.
And the Soviets, when they came during the war, since it was a pact, they had no fighting, so it was a peaceful exchange.
The Soviets immediately nationalized all the homes of the better-to-do people, and they--
That was in '41.
Up until '39.
I was in gymnasium at that time, and I returned from the gymnasium of Rowne.
In Rowne, right.

After I finished the sixth grade in the tarbut in Hoszcza, I-- I went to study one year in gymnasium, and that's all we were able to-- to complete, because right then, the war started, and we came to the town of Hoszcza back.
And--
The-- actually, you know, the-- the Soviets occupied at that time already.
Soviets occupied the-- '39 was the war, you know, the blitzkrieg.
And when it was over, Poland was [INAUDIBLE] occupied by the Soviets.
And then, of course, there was no schooling in Hebrew, and the teachers that knew the language say Polish or-- that they were of no use.
Hebrew in Polish was not taught anymore in Soviet schools.
They established-- they called it [POLISH], which means a 10-year school program.
And they had-- they had Russian, and for a very brief time, Yiddish.
Changed immediately to Ukrainian, and that was the two languages.
Seventh and eighth grade I went there.
Back in Hoszcza.
Yes, we left-- I left Rowne.
Yes, back in Hoszcza.
As I told you, the house-- the homes were nationalized and so was the business.
And to the home moved in a Soviet citizen, Soviet citizen that were really in the government.
And they would say--
No--
--we had to move out.
Yes, we had to move out.
And we moved out into the outskirts of Hoszcza.
A farmer was renting part of his house to another Jewish family and us.
At that time, we were only my mother, I, and my sister Leah because my sister Paula was married, and she lived in an apartment with her husband, Peter.
She was librarian and he was our teacher-- my-- my teacher and my husband's teacher.
He was very good in the Russian, he, so he was the Russian language teacher.
And he was also in woodwork very handy, so that he taught the students.
And the Soviets were there.
I did not care for them too much.
My mother hated them.
She was-- had a brother in [POLISH], and he came to visit.
And he-- and we lived at that time on the outskirts of [?
Hoszcza ?].
He never saw our new house.
He told her to draw the curtains and-- and to close the windows so he could tell of the horrors of the Soviet regime.
And she told us a little bit not to scare us too much.
With that apartment we had two rooms-- a bedroom, a living room, and a kitchen.
And we stayed there until Germany declared war on Russia.
And that was the spring of '42.
We had the yard goods left over and we had them hidden, but we could not get to the house to take them out because they were occupied by the Soviet citizens, Soviet--
I think we brought the bedding, I suppose.
A bed.
Not too much, but enough to, you know, for the living room.
We had to leave most of the furniture in there.
And they--
Yes.
The town was-- the school was in two places-- not anymore where the tarbut was, but closer where we had to move.
It was called [POLISH] which means beyond the-- the river.
And that's where the school was.
The Germans declared war, and that was in year of '40-- '41?
I guess so.
And the-- the-- the Russians started retreating.
My sister Paula and her husband, they left by trucks.
Took some of their clothes with them and they left for Russia.
She-- very possible that we would have been able to-- to evacuate with them, but I don't think my mother wanted to go to Russia, to the Soviet Union, I would call it.
The very first thing was the Germans bombed.
They did not bomb the outskirts of Hoszcza, only the town of Hoszcza.
Our home was burned, many other homes were burned, and there were a lot of casualties.
If they were alive, they were wounded.
My mother and I and my sister, we hid in the-- in the-- you know, the weed was high already and that's where we hid.
And the bombs were all around, with exploding all around us.
And we were OK.
The-- the people said that the reason they did not bomb the villages is because the Germans pointed out-- the Ukrainians and the Germans pointed out where the Jewish people lived.
This is why Hoszcza suffered such casualties.
And their homes were spared and so where their lives.
Yes.
Yes, we all wore-- yes, men, women.
I-- since I was 17, I, too, wore a star.
We could not live on the outskirts.
It was too dangerous.
We worried very much the Ukrainians would harm us.
And they were collaborators hand in hand with the Germans.
They-- that's why we moved to that pharmacy, which were my cousins pharmacy, although my uncle wasn't alive anymore, but she, his daughter, was a pharmacist and so was her husband.
And they were very lenient with him, the Germans and the Ukrainians, because it was the only pharmacy in the area and they were very, very capable people.
So we had a room there.
Another uncle of mine had-- had a room with two children, and another aunt lived not too far, but not-- but not in that pharmacy home.
The pharmacy was in town.
In the middle of town, right across from the stores.
We were safe until a point.
This is when they only came in and made out new documents.
They selected the committee, and the committee was the one that had to deliver to them furs and jewelry from the-- from the townspeople.
The two warm fur coats of my father, of course.
And she had no fur coats, but whoever had did not hesitate to give them.
A judenrat.
It was several of the townspeople were in the judenrat.
No, they were selected.

As soon as the Germans came in and after the bombing-- I have to go back a little bit-- 10 of the most prominent people of Hoszcza were taken, and there were rumors that they are alive, that they're working somewhere, but very shortly we knew that on the outskirts of Hoszcza they were all killed.
I don't think we know where the graves are, but we had an idea where they were killed.
Those were not just the Nazis, not just the SS that did it, but the Ukrainians also because the prominent Jews, if they would remain, they would really, really punish them later after the war.
So they made sure that none of them were alive.
And the town had to work [INAUDIBLE] men and women.
And I would say people that were of age worked in the-- in that same garrison where the park was.
Now SS and military were there. it was just an exchange of roles from a Polish army to a Russian army to a German army, except that it was-- we were in great danger from them like never before.
No, my mother stayed home and she cooked for the family.
The pharmacy-- my sister worked in the pharmacy, and I helped with my-- taking care of the little cousin, my cousin's little boy.
We very seldom ventured out to the-- to the village to get food because also the villages, especially our maids, they had a lot of our belongings, our possessions.
And when it came to claim them, they weren't always happy to give them back or they wouldn't give them back at all.
Yes.
Well, when the time came in the spring, we heard rumors that they are digging graves, and the name of this small village was [POLISH].
Right.
They wasted no time.
And the rumors were true.
So my sister Leah heard that the town of [POLISH] had already an-- an action-- action-- so she figured it's safe to go there.
It's about 30 kilometers from our town.
My mother and I, we were really planning to hide, but there was no special place to hide, like an attic or something like that.
We were in the fields instead.
And then they--
We slept out in the fields that particular night that was close already when the graves were already.
And one evening, we heard from someone that used to work with my brother in [POLISH], and he reported that tomorrow it will be the-- the action.
And go and hide and run.
And since he knew my sister, she was the one that told everyone that was able to--
So we went to the-- to the village.
Villagers walked from village to village, but hid mainly.
That was in the weed or in-- or in the fields.
And that morning, we were not home already.
And every-- all the Jews from the town--
No, she left for [POLISH].
And all the people from the town were roused very early in the morning and chased out.
And that was a distance of at least 10 kilometers.
And they were herded-- men and the children.
Everyone was there at the graves.
The-- the town was-- was empty.
Not just the German SS was there, the Ukrainians were helping them, too.
And they were all herded to those graves in [POLISH].
Graves and woods, actually.
[POLISH] they were called, from the name of-- [POLISH] is a-- [SIGH] a tree--
Not birch, no, but that is green all year-round.
An evergreen.
[POLISH].
And they-- they were-- we heard the shot.
My mother and I landed in a cemetery not too far from where the killing was going on.
And then at around-- it was late in the afternoon.
We were found.
And--
The SS and the Ukrainians.
And they said that the action's over, you can go back to town.
Of course.
Who else would be hiding in a-- at a cemetery?
We both didn't look Jewish, but every Ukrainian had a nose for a Jew.
He can spot them miles away.
We really have no idea, but since they are so precise, the Germans, it was 4:05, and they finished their job.
From that grave I heard later told that three people were taken out-- the pharmacist.
The Ukrainians recognized him and he was very much a useful Jew, so he was freed.
He was not shot.
His wife and little boy were hiding, so they didn't find him.
The photographer was taken out, and he was laughing and dancing, and they knew that he was crazy, so he was shot.
And the smith-- the smith, the blacksmith of the town, was a Jew also.
He and his family were in the graves so [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they-- the Ukrainians wanted him to be freed.
So he said to the commandant, if you free my family, then I will go free also, and they didn't agree, so they were all shot.
This story was told by [POLISH].
[POLISH] was a man that his sisters were-- sister and brother were my schoolmates.
They did not survive.
They perished.
But he had very good friends between the Ukrainians, and he survived.
After the war, he brought the bones, some of the bones, from the-- from that forest to Israel.
Israel was established already in 1948.
And when he came to Israel, he brought with him the bones, and they are buried now on the outskirts of Tel Aviv.
So we came back to town.
The town was really in terrible shape.
Terrible shape.
The town was-- was-- the homes were emptied of possessions, opened windows.
But we came to the pharmacy and found out that my cousin, the one that hid, that was the pharmacist also-- she's alive.
And a lot of Jewish people were killed at that first action.
After the action, it was-- nights were-- were just really sleepless.
Sleepless.
And the dreams were awful.
And many times, we just didn't know what to do, where to hide, what to do.
And my mother inquired between the customers of ours-- such trusted customers and trusted maids-- that if anything should happen again, would they-- would they take us in?
Would they shelter us?
We really never had a definite answer from them.
My cousin, the pharmacist, made out Aryan papers.
And-- How did she get them?
She had the connection because of-- because of the pharmacy.
Because the-- the-- even the SS, even the Germans, needed some assistance with pharmacology.
And they helped them, you know, with medicine.
And the Ukrainians, the one that was not in the Judenrat but they were in the-- in the-- in their committees, they also were very much helped.
No.
Herself and her husband.
We stayed in the pharmacy--
Until the fall.
Until the fall when the new action started.
Right, when the-- when the rumors of the second action is going to be.
